# Ben Sprung-Keyser

Economist
Assistant Professor, Business Economics and Public Policy
The Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania

Email: bsprungk@wharton.upenn.edu
Website: https://bsprungkeyser.com

Research fields: public economics, labor economics.
Co-director at Policy Impacts (https://policyimpacts.org).
PhD in Economics, Harvard University.

---

## Publications

### A Welfare Analysis of Policies Impacting Climate Change
Authors: Robert Hahn, Nathaniel Hendren, Robert Metcalfe, Ben Sprung-Keyser
Journal: Forthcoming, American Economic Review
Paper: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/MVPF_Climate_Policy_AER_Draft.pdf
Appendix: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/MVPF_Climate_Policy_AER_Appendix.pdf
Executive Summary: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/MVPF_Climate_Exec_Summary.pdf

We study the welfare impacts of 96 climate-related tax and spending policies. We extend and apply the marginal value of public funds (MVPF) framework, most notably providing a new method for incorporating learning-by-doing spillovers. We find subsidies for the production of clean energy (such as wind production tax credits) have higher MVPFs than all other subsidies in our sample, including EV subsidies. Conservation nudges have large MVPFs when targeting regions with dirty grids. Fuel taxes and cap-and-trade policies are highly efficient means of raising revenue. We also construct traditional cost-per-ton estimates and compare and contrast the lessons they provide.

### A Welfare Analysis of Tax Audits Across the Income Distribution
Authors: William C. Boning, Nathaniel Hendren, Ben Sprung-Keyser, Ellen Stuart
Journal: Quarterly Journal of Economics, 140(1), 63-112, 2025
Paper: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/Tax_Audits_BHSKS.pdf
Appendix: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/Tax_Audits_Appendix.pdf
Executive Summary: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/audits_executive_summary.pdf

We estimate the returns to IRS audits of taxpayers across the income distribution. We find an additional $1 spent auditing taxpayers above the 90th income percentile yields more than $12 in revenue, while audits of below-median income taxpayers yield $5. We construct our estimates by drawing from comprehensive internal accounting information and audit-level enforcement logs.

### A Unified Welfare Analysis of Government Policies
Authors: Nathaniel Hendren, Ben Sprung-Keyser
Journal: Quarterly Journal of Economics, 135(3), 1209-1318, 2020
Paper: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/MVPF_Mar_2020_QJE.pdf
Appendix: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/MVPF_Appendix.pdf

We conduct a comparative welfare analysis of 133 historical policy changes over the past half-century in the United States, focusing on policies in social insurance, education and job training, taxes and cash transfers, and in-kind transfers. For each policy, we use existing causal estimates to calculate the benefit that each policy provides its recipients and the policy's net cost, inclusive of long-term effects on the government's budget.

---

## Working Papers

### The Economic Geography of Lifecycle Human Capital Accumulation: The Competing Effects of Labor Markets and Childhood Environments
Authors: Ben Sprung-Keyser, Sonya Porter
Status: Job Market Paper
Paper: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/BSK_JMP_Linked.pdf

We examine how place shapes the production of human capital across the lifecycle. We ask: do those places that most effectively produce human capital in childhood also have local labor markets that do so in adulthood? We find there is a tradeoff between those places that most effectively produce human capital in childhood and the local labor markets that do so in adulthood. Each 1-rank increase in earnings due to adult labor market exposure trades off with a 0.43 rank decrease in earnings due to the local childhood environment.

### State Taxes, Migration, and Capital Gains Realizations
Authors: Lucas Goodman, Ben Sprung-Keyser
Status: Revision Requested, AEJ:Policy
Paper: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/GSK_Capital_Gains_Resubmission%20(2).pdf
Appendix: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/Appendix_GSK_Capital_Gains_Resubmission.pdf

We analyze the impact of state capital gains taxes on migration and realization. We find the probability of migrating to a zero-tax state before realization rises with potential tax savings. Savings in zero-tax states drive $2 billion in annual realizations by former residents of positive-tax states.

### The Radius of Economic Opportunity: Evidence from Migration and Local Labor Markets
Authors: Ben Sprung-Keyser, Nathaniel Hendren, Sonya Porter
Status: Center for Economic Studies Working Paper Series, US Census Bureau, CES-22-27
Paper: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/Migration_vSI.pdf

We examine the geographic incidence of local labor market growth across locations of childhood residence. Our results imply that for a 2 rank point increase in annual wages in a given commuting zone, approximately 99% of wage gains flow to those who would have resided in the CZ in the absence of the wage change. For many individuals, the "radius of economic opportunity" is quite narrow.

---

## Notes

### The Case for Using the MVPF in Empirical Welfare Analysis
Authors: Nathaniel Hendren, Ben Sprung-Keyser
Status: NBER Working Paper, No. 30029
Paper: https://bsprungkeyser.com/assets/MVPF_Case_vFinal.pdf

This paper outlines the case for using the Marginal Value of Public Funds (MVPF) in empirical welfare analysis. It compares the MVPF approach with more traditional welfare metrics such as the Cost-Benefit Ratio and the Net Social Benefits criterion.

---
